data.title: ROBO Vs. BOTZ: Which Is The Best "Robotics" ETF?
data.description:
What is Robotics? For investors, it's likely the next major trend in AI, or simply an intriguing application of AI technology. More accurately, AI will probably accelerate the development of robotics. However, when we think about robotics, many of us envision "Asimov-style" robots—machines that could replace humans or, in some dystopian scenarios, even threaten humanity. This is often the image that comes to mind when imagining the future of robotics.

In reality, robotics (for now) is something quite different from the vision Tesla presented at the WeRobot event in October (I'm somewhat critical of it). My perspective is much more grounded, so for now, investing in robotics feels more like participating in the consumer stock segment—a segment I want to highlight in my portfolio.

But which ETF is the best way to invest in this space, and which one truly tracks the real evolution of the robotics industry? The two with the largest assets under management (AUM) are the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (NASDAQ:BOTZ) and the ROBO Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (NYSEARCA:ROBO). Lastly, a fund with less AUM but often discussed in market forums is the ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF (ARKQ). I’ve already hinted at my choice: it’s ROBO.

What robotics really is

If you’re thinking that the closest thing to the robotics market is the robots proposed by Tesla, then, like me, you’re probably a big fan of sci-fi movies like Star Wars or Star Trek (though I’d like to clarify that Star Trek is worse than Star Wars). But unfortunately, that’s not what robotics looks like right now (though that doesn’t mean it won’t be the future of robotics).

Let me give you some excellent examples of what robotics actually looks like today by listing some of the most prominent robotics solutions available on the market.

  • Let’s start with Fanuc, one of the leading players in industrial robotics. They have already installed over 24 million robots worldwide. The FANUC R-2000iC is one of FANUC's best-selling industrial robots: it handles materials, welds points, assembles, and moves heavy loads. The demand for industrial robots is continuously growing, more than 500,000 new industrial robots were installed in 2022. This figure is expected to grow by 6% annually until 2025.
  • In the healthcare sector, the da Vinci system by Intuitive Surgical has revolutionized minimally invasive surgery. Since its launch, over 10 million surgeries have been performed using this system. It is now used in more than 5,500 hospitals around the world, offering greater precision and faster recovery for patients.
  • Home robots are also becoming more common, though they don’t quite resemble the humanoid robots we see in sci-fi. A prime example is the Roomba vacuum cleaner by iRobot, which has sold over 40 million units worldwide. And this is just the beginning—the global home robotics market is expected to be worth 30 billion dollars by 2026, with an annual growth rate of 20%.

In short, we’ve been living with sophisticated robotics for decades, and their applications are clear. When you invest in robotics, you’re primarily investing in practical solutions like these, which are much closer to "modern vacuum cleaners" than AI-driven robots taking over the world (for now).

Why I Would Not Invest in the BOTZ ETF

The two largest ETFs by AUM in this space are BOTZ and ROBO. While they might seem like similar options at first glance, a deeper comparison reveals some interesting differences.

Let’s start with BOTZ, the largest ETF focused on robotics. It tracks the Indxx Global Robotics & Artificial Intelligence Thematic Index. Right away, it's clear that Global X doesn’t offer a purely robotics-focused solution. Instead, it combines both AI and robotics, as if these two fields are closely intertwined. While I believe AI will significantly boost the robotics industry, it's important to recognize that, at present, they remain quite distinct.

Let’s take a look at BOTZ’s holdings breakdown:

Over 13% of the ETF's exposure is allocated to NVIDIA Corp. The two largest holdings in BOTZ's portfolio make up over 24% of the ETF's weight, while the top 10 holdings account for around 65-70% of its total exposure. This concentrated holding structure comes with a Total Expense Ratio (TER) of 0.68%. In my opinion, that’s quite high.

When I see this kind of composition, I ask myself: are you really trying to gain exposure to robotics or just AI? Because, as you probably know, NVIDIA is easily found in nearly every tech ETF. So, why pay 0.68% annually for exposure that is very similar to a general tech ETF? It might make more sense to find a broader technology ETF to gain exposure to AI, since AI will impact the tech industry overall. Meanwhile, you can focus on finding a more specific robotics ETF for exposure to the evolution of robotics—something you won’t find in a traditional tech ETF.

For example, with a lower expense ratio, I believe a better option is the iShares U.S. Technology ETF (IYW), which has 14% exposure to NVIDIA but offers a more balanced exposure to the disruptive aspects of AI, at a lower cost.

If you're looking for a thematic solution in the robotics sector, welcome to the wild. There’s no single "right" solution here. This is my personal strategy, which I created using the Seeking Alpha tool.

Peers Analysis

Starting from that table ...

It’s easy to see that the ETF with the largest AUM is ROBO, but ROBO also has the highest expense ratio, which is very high, even for a thematic ETF. You may have also noticed that I included the Franklin Disruptive Commerce ETF (BUYZ) in the peer comparison table; this was just to show how stocks in the robotics sector tend to follow the trends of consumer stocks, even though, from a general perspective, robotics should be more aligned with the technology sector. Indeed, over the past five years, the total return has shown an incredible correlation with the disruptive consumer segment .

In terms of total return alone, the ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF (ARKQ) is the clear winner. So, what’s inside this ETF? The fund follows its benchmark, the S&P 500 TR USD, which aims to replicate the stock market performance of the robotics and “automation technology” theme.

This is fascinating because, in my opinion, the robotics industry is closely tied to autonomous technology. But what exactly does ARK mean when they talk about automation? Let’s dive into the holding breakdown.

12.05% is allocated to Tesla, and 61% of the total exposure is concentrated in the top 10 holdings, with a total of 36 holdings and an expense ratio of 0.75%. At the same time, ARKQ provides good exposure to the U.S. stock market; however, we’ve already seen that a significant portion of the current robotics market exists outside the U.S. Before ruling it out, let’s take a closer look at ROBO.

ROBO has strong exposure to automation technology, as it aims to track the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation TR USD (its benchmark). What’s the strategy behind it?

Honestly, I see something here that I really like: nearly 90 holdings, with only 17.86% of the ETF portfolio exposed to the top 10 holdings. There’s no overwhelming concentration, and to me, that indicates a well-diversified fund. Its AUM is over $1 billion, which is a good sign, while the others (apart from BOTZ) are below $1 billion. This ETF provides solid exposure to the current robotics and automation technology stock market and is more globally diversified than its peers.

Even though it underperforms compared to its peers, in my opinion, this is because the global robotics and automation stock market is moving sideways, with a few exceptions like Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) and others. However, overall, the sector is experiencing stagnation.

Risk

The only negative aspect is the high Expense Ratio, at 0.95%. Over the long term, this will likely impact price appreciation. That’s a risk, but if we compare the performance of its benchmark index with the S&P 500 and the MSCI ACWI index, we can see that the performance charts are nearly linear.

At the same time, investing in ROBO could come with a significant opportunity cost. As can be easily seen in the charts, compared to the S&P 500, there has been a noticeable performance gap in the last two years, even though over the past decade, ROBO's performance was quite similar to that of the S&P 500. Some might refer to this gap as "alpha," depending on your point of view.

Conclusion

To sum up, I believe that ROBO is the best solution currently available in the ETF market for a well-diversified and globally positioned investment in the robotics and autonomous technology sectors. The expense ratio of nearly 1% is quite high, even compared to its peers, but from a portfolio perspective, in my opinion, it’s not overly destructive to value. In fact, it’s preferable to other ETFs that are heavily weighted toward companies that can easily be found in more general ETFs or through simple stock picking.

data.title: Sonos Will Outride The App Fiasco But The Damage Makes Them A Poor Investment
data.description:
Thesis

Over the years, Sonos' (NASDAQ:SONO) capable engineering team, along with a few acquisitions, have led to releases of products that have been well regarded by customers. Their customer image and their engineering know-how created opportunities to leverage their capabilities and enter new product markets, such as the headphone market, which they have done successfully (judging by reviews, not sales). In their twenty years of existence they have not achieved stable profitability, yet, according to the CEO, the company is at the beginning of a new product cycle which will allow them to benefit from years of R&D expenditure and create sales growth, whilst expanding the brand's image. This could have been the road to long-term profitability. However, the release of a dysfunctional app earlier this year has significantly harmed the brand image in the eyes of customers as they have been unable to use their premium products. With this damage has come a decline in the stock price - can the company outride this reputation damage and find a path to profitability for investors?

This article argues that the company is likely to go onto create growth again and begin the slow process of rebuilding its brand image, but the profits are too uncertain and too far in the future, given the current stock price, to justify an attractive investment.

Business Overview

Sonos, a leader in wireless audio technology, is renowned for its premium, multiroom speaker systems and soundbars. Founded in 2002, the company has consistently focused on innovation, creating products that seamlessly integrate with a wide range of streaming services and smart home devices. Known for its Trueplay room-tuning technology and high-fidelity sound, Sonos has expanded its offerings into commercial audio and recently introduced Bluetooth-enabled speakers. Sonos continues to develop in both consumer and professional markets, leveraging its engineering excellence and design.

Engineering Excellence and Customer Reputation

Sonos' reputation for engineering excellence and high-quality audio products has lead to significant customer loyalty. Products like the Sonos Era 300 and Era 100 have been praised for their cutting-edge features, such as Trueplay room correction and Dolby Atmos support, which allow customers to fine-tune their audio experience. These innovations not only create a differentiated product in a crowded market but also foster a strong connection with users who value premium sound quality. Such features not only attract first-time buyers but also encourage repeat purchases and brand loyalty, providing a stable and growing consumer base​. Ace, Sonos' first pair of headphones, have received very high ratings, highlighting the company's ability to enter new segments.

This high level of customer satisfaction, combined with Sonos' seamless integration across multiple devices and platforms, gives the company a competitive edge when entering new markets. For instance, Sonos' expansion into commercial audio and other categories will likely benefit from the brand's existing positive reputation. Loyal customers are more inclined to adopt new Sonos offerings, knowing the brand's commitment to innovation and sound quality. This kind of consumer trust can lower the barriers to entry in new markets and speed up market penetration​.

Furthermore, Sonos' long-standing reputation for building products that last and delivering a premium user experience supports its potential for growth. For investors, this reputation not only helps retain existing customers but also attracts new consumers, particularly in international and commercial segments where brand trust is critical. This ongoing customer loyalty and positive perception provide Sonos with a strategic advantage in maintaining pricing power and achieving sustained profitability​.

Market Potential

The market for Sonos' core product, high-quality wireless speakers and home theatre systems, is unlikely to experience significant growth rates in the near term. This is largely due to market saturation following the pandemic boom, which saw consumers upgrading their home entertainment systems while confined indoors. Growth in this category has since slowed, with Sonos reporting negative growth rates. However, this is more reflective of market saturation and temporary macroeconomic factors like inflation and the cost of living squeeze than long-term trends. Over the long run, the market is expected to grow steadily, driven by technological advancements such as enhanced audio experiences and the rise of spatial audio, along with increasing global incomes and demand for premium entertainment systems in emerging markets.

Whilst there hasn't been significant growth in the emerging market for Sonos, if we look at the growth of large-screen TVs, we see there is opportunity. The market for large-screen TVs, particularly those used in home theatre setups, is experiencing robust growth in emerging markets, driven by rising disposable incomes and a growing middle class. The Asia-Pacific region, led by countries like China and India, is expected to see strong demand for 65-inch and larger TVs, with the overall smart TV market projected to grow at a CAGR of 13.8% through 2029​. The global home theatre market is also expected to expand by 9.08% from 2024 to 2028, reflecting a broader trend towards in-home entertainment and premium audio-visual systems​.

Sonos has built a strong foundation of engineering expertise within the audio technology sector. Its proprietary Trueplay sound-tuning software, which customises sound output based on the room's acoustics, is just one example of its technical prowess. The company also excels in wireless multiroom audio systems, a complex task requiring wireless signal processing, low-latency communication, and robust software integration across devices. Furthermore, Sonos has invested heavily in patent-backed acoustic innovations-for instance, its 2022 acquisition of Mayht's transducer technology, which reduces the size of audio drivers without sacrificing sound quality. These technologies provide a competitive edge in expanding to new categories, including wearables, in-car audio systems, and smart home integration.

Expanding into new product markets allows Sonos to leverage these core competencies. The company recently ventured into the headphones market, a natural progression given its acoustic expertise. The premium over-the ear headphone market is worth $5 billion, and therefore represents a significant source of potential growth for the company in itself. Additionally, Sonos' partnerships in the automotive sector-most notably with Audi-demonstrate the potential for further expansion into in-car audio systems. The global in-car audio system market is expected to grow to$14.4 billion by 2030, with a CAGR of 5.8% from 2024 onwards. This growth is driven by the increasing demand for premium in-car audio systems and advancements like 3D sound and voice recognition​.

The company is trying to tap into the commercial audio solutions market, such as in-store sound systems, where high-end audio integration is increasingly in demand as businesses look for premium solutions. The commercial audio market provides audio solutions tailored for public and business environments, including retail spaces, offices, conference rooms, and large venues like stadiums. These systems often focus on delivering clear, high-quality sound over larger areas, integrating components like amplifiers, speakers, and mixing consoles. Increasingly, this market is driven by the demand for networked audio solutions (e.g., AV over IP) and digitalisation, making systems more flexible and scalable. Sonos has entered this market with Sonos Pro, a subscription service aimed at businesses, but the company faces challenges in fully leveraging its brand awareness and navigating unfamiliar customer preferences. It's engineering expertise in sound and multiroom capabilities gives it a competitive edge, but because the commercial sector is crowded with established players, scaling in this market will require significant investment in R&D and partnerships. Therefore, their ability to develop innovative solutions to specific business cases will remain key. I am not sure with their current size, tailored for consumer products, whether they will be able to take advantage of this, but it does create a path for long-term growth.

Sonos' history of product innovation demonstrates the company's commitment to aggressively pursuing growth. From launching the first multiroom wireless sound system in 2005 to its most recent products, the Era 300 and Ace, Sonos has continually pushed the boundaries of audio technology. CEO Patrick Spence has articulated this drive for expansion, stating,"We are at the onset of a multi-year product cycle as we harvest the benefits of our investments to enter new categories and launch new products"​. This proactive approach, backed by a robust R&D pipeline, positions Sonos to continue exploring new opportunities and drive future growth in an increasingly competitive market.

App Fiasco

The Sonos app fiasco occurred when the company released a major update to its app in mid-2024, which removed key features, particularly affecting Android users. Many customers felt blindsided as the changes significantly impacted their existing systems, which led to widespread backlash. CEO Patrick Spence defended the redesign, stating it improved the overall user experience, but the lack of clear communication and feature removal left many long-term users frustrated, damaging trust and customer loyalty​.

According to this article management ignored the employees who raised concerns about bugs in the new app. There was, apparently, "screaming and shouting." Ignoring employees' concerns is a very bad trait for management to have. However, considering some of these employees were let go in a recent round of layoffs, we should take the comments with a degree of skepticism. I am not saying that management didn't ignore employees; there is just a tendency of ex-employees to exaggerate the flaws of management. Nevertheless, it reflects very poorly on management that this significant damage to the brand's reputation could have been avoided easily. I doubt this mistake will be made again, given the fact that the CEO has accepted responsibility for it and apologised. Therefore, the main question is the degree of damage caused and whether it will be forgotten.

The dysfunctionality of the app does undermine the company's reputation of engineering excellence. Other companies like Apple and Microsoft have released in their histories poorly performing apps and products but have recovered without their reputations being irreparably damaged. What is different with the Sonos case is that this app was central to the user experience, and its inability to function left many users with speakers they had purchased essentially useless.

The Sonos app has improved significantly since its problematic update earlier in 2024, with 90% of the missing features now restored. The company has worked to address critical issues and reintroduce legacy features that customers requested. Ongoing updates are planned, including improvements to system stability, volume control, playlist editing, and UI enhancements, particularly for Android users. Sonos has committed to regular updates, aiming to make the overall experience better than before.

Yet when I look at the App Store, the iOS App Store, which supposedly holds the better app, there is nothing but one-star reviews all saying how terrible the app is. They criticise the company harshly, pointing out that one spends a fortune on their speakers and then their functionality is severely limited by the app.

Sonos speaker owners generally need to use the Sonos app for setup and for accessing some features, such as Trueplay room tuning, multiroom audio control, and streaming services integration. However, certain newer speakers, like the Sonos Era 100 and Era 300, now offer Bluetooth support, which allows users to stream directly from their devices without using the app. While the app enhances the experience, it is no longer strictly required for basic functions on these newer models.

One of the reasons to buy a Sonos was that they work seamlessly. When setting up a home theatre or sound system, most people aren't technicians and just want it to work quickly and not have problems. This aspect of the company's reputation has essentially been worn off by this error. Further than that, the frustration caused to clients by not having their speakers work and, at the beginning when the errors surfaced, the poor communication with customers has led to a significant impairment of brand value. It is a shame because the company image was flawless essentially prior to this. We can expect the core sales - sales from customers upgrading to new Sonos products - to be reduced for years to come. And therefore, the company will have to rely more on growth to achieve profitability.

Financial Results

While much of this report focuses on Sonos' growth potential, that growth must translate into profits to be meaningful for investors. Despite being founded in 2002, Sonos only achieved profitability during the booming years of the home theatre market, which is concerning. If Sonos cannot finance the development of new products from its own earnings, shareholders may face dilution, as the company would need to raise capital rather than delivering capital gains. However, they do have approximately $230 million of cash on their balance sheet to finance development and operations.

On a more positive note, CEO Patrick Spence has stated,"We are at the onset of a multiyear product cycle as we harvest the benefits of our investments to enter new categories and launch new products." Product development in the audio sector typically spans two to three years, meaning a substantial portion of the costs associated with development has already been incurred in recent years. This suggests that future growth might come without corresponding increases in R&D expenses, which could allow Sonos to maintain profitability going forward. Additionally supporting the case for profitability is their increase in their gross margin to 48%, from a low of 42% in 2019. This will be more important going forward relative to controlling the costs of R&D.

However, the recent app fiasco could delay or prevent this profitability, depending on the long-term impact on consumer trust. Sonos' business model relies on building brand loyalty over time and releasing innovative products that reinforce its reputation, a process requiring consistency. One significant mistake, like the app issue, can undermine this strategy, potentially delaying the company's path to sustainable profits.

Valuation

Despite the problems with the app, the company will survive over the foreseeable future and likely return to sales growth due to its expansion into different categories. Its brand reputation has been tarnished, but its engineering capabilities are still intact, and the company will have to begin restoring its reputation. The question, then, is whether or not the current valuation makes the business an attractive investment.

Without consistent profitability, and relying on growth from new segments, it is quite difficult to value this company. I think that it is simpler to look at it backwards. The stock is down 25% this year to a market capitalisation of $1.55 billion. Given the risk and growth potential, what kind of price to earnings would make this attractive? The CAGR in revenue has been less than 8% on average since 2017. This should increase as new products are released. But with such volatility and losses, I think the cost of capital, given current market rates, should be around 15%. This is less than venture capital. Let's assume the growth rate remains 8%. Taking the price to earnings as one over the discount rate minus the growth rate, we get 14.3.

So with the market cap at $1.55 billion and the PE at 14.3, we'd need to see current net earnings at approximately $110 million to be a fair-valued investment. That's in between the boom years of 2021 and 2022, and I doubt, especially with the app problems, and current losses, the business will achieve that for a number of years to come if they ever do.

Summary

The company has built a business based on engineering developments that has led to a strong market image. Yet, they have in their twenty years of existence and growth been able to translate this into consistent profitability. New product launches and entering new markets by leveraging their technical know-how is a possible avenue towards that profitability. However, the damage done to their reputation with the app fiasco will undermine sales from their core customer base and their ability to capitalise on their image with regard to further growth. This has pushed back the potential for profitability and, given the risks, this leads to likely poor overall investment returns.

data.title: I Am Loading Up On RQI With Big Income: Yield 7%
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

Rarely does the market move swiftly in one direction continuously. If you take a step back and examine the history of the market, while it looks like an upward climb, there are frequent dips, divots, and drops along the way. Likewise, when a sector is recovering, it is very unlikely that that recovery will be a clean upward climb. There will be moments of upward momentum and moments of consolidation, followed by renewed upward momentum once again.

REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts) are a sector that benefits from lower interest rates. Real estate and debt are intertwined in the economy. Banks love real estate as collateral for loans because it is a physical asset that has durable value. Meanwhile, the primary business model in real estate is to invest large amounts of capital and receive a small but steady return through rent.

Whenever you have a business model that involves large amounts of capital upfront in exchange for a stable stream of cash flow, the use of leverage will be very attractive. In the world of publicly traded companies, where one REIT has to compete with other REITs to attract investment dollars, leverage is essential. As a result, most REITs will carry debt somewhere in the ballpark of 40-70% of their gross real estate assets. Modern REITs are excellent at laddering their debt and usually rely heavily on fixed-rate debt with 5-10-year terms. So year to year, interest rates don't mean much for the bottom line. However, when investors see large amounts of debt, there is certainly the perception that high interest rates are “bad” and low rates are “good.”

In the stock market, perception is sometimes as important as reality. For the past few years, we have enjoyed a massive sell-off among REITs as the market feared high interest expenses. However, it's been a high expense that most REITs haven't actually paid, and the amount of debt most REITs saw maturing in 2023 and 2024 was minimal.

After two years of declines, we saw REITs rally strongly in Q3, fueled by the rumor that the Fed would cut rates. Prices are still well below the January 2022 peak, but the recovery is underway.

After the Fed cut, we've seen REIT prices pull back as the market is assessing that the cutting cycle will slow down and cut only 25 bps at the next meeting. Does it matter? Not really. Fundamentally, no REIT will be materially impacted whether the Fed cuts the next 50 bps in one meeting or over two meetings. One month isn't going to fundamentally alter the returns for REITs that typically have bond maturity ladders going out 10 years.

We view this as an opportunity to buy REITs before they make the next leg up and achieve a full recovery.

One Of The Best REIT-focused Funds

While there are many great options to choose from, one of our favorites is Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (NYSE:RQI), yielding 7%. RQI invests in REITs on a leveraged basis, usually leveraged at 25-30% of assets. As a leveraged closed-end fund, or CEF, RQI benefits from lower interest rates directly, on top of whatever improved performance its investments experience. However, like its investments, whether rates decline quickly or slowly is not a huge deal. 81% of RQI's leverage is fixed using interest rate swaps, with an average term of over two years. Source.

As a result, interest rate cuts have only a small positive impact immediately, as only 19% of variable debt is reduced immediately. However, declining rates over the next two years is definitely a benefit, as it will reduce interest expense as those interest rate swaps mature.

RQI's holdings are a Who's Who of REITs. If REITs had Oscars, these are the companies that would be walking the red carpet. Source.

Sure, you can find someone on the internet who will talk trash about these companies, but if you are talking to people who know the business, these are companies that will be widely recognized as top-tier.

Why not just buy these companies directly? After all, the top 10 make up over 50% of RQI's holdings, so that is a fairly significant concentration. All of these are fantastic companies, so if you want to buy some or all of them directly, go for it. Whether to buy RQI or individual picks falls back on your investment goals.

A CEF provides a few core advantages:

  1. Instant diversification and management. You can manage a portfolio of REITs, or you can just buy RQI and let Cohen & Steers manage it for you. C&S has a strong track record in this sector.
  2. Access to cheap leverage. RQI is paying 2.4% on its leverage, can you get leverage that cheap? Leverage amplifies returns in both directions. If you believe REITs are going to go up, then being leveraged is an advantage; if you believe that REITs are at risk of going down, then perhaps you want to be more conservative, and RQI's sister fund, Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty (RFI) provides a similar portfolio without leverage.
  3. A managed distribution. CEFs are required to pay out substantially all of their taxable gains, including capital gains, as dividends. As a result, over the long run, a CEF will distribute substantially all the total return it generates as dividends. For income investors like us, it is very convenient to have a fund that produces a high level of cash flow, rather than managing a portfolio that has a lower yield.

This pause in the REIT recovery is a great opportunity to jump on board the RQI train before it goes on another run.

Conclusion

With RQI, we can benefit from the continued expected recovery within the REIT sector, all while enjoying the benefits that the CEF wrapper provides shareholders. This would be the higher income that is paid out, the ability to buy at a discount or a premium depending on the situation, and the expertise of the management team watching over that specific section of your portfolio. When Mr. Market moves sectors based on sentiment alone, you can benefit by having an outsourced management team that manages those investments for you. RQI provides that to you, or even RFI if you're more conservative.

When it comes to retirement, I don't recommend chasing every single sector all the time. In fact, you shouldn't be chasing anything at all in your retirement. Instead, I recommend that you carefully and diligently build an income stream that will benefit regardless of what the market is doing. What that means is buying wonderful opportunities at discounted prices and a strong outlook. In this instance, you can buy into the real estate sector through a fund wrapper and benefit not only from higher levels of consistent income, but also the capital gains that are due to come down the road. These are the types of investments you can hold for decades to come. The underlying values of the properties that these different REITs hold will rise with inflation, a constant phenomenon that battles against the value of your fixed Social Security income or other comparable sources.

At the end of the day, an abundance of income that floods into your account and puts out the fire of your expenses is precisely what you need. This is something our unique Income Method can provide for you. That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Teen Employment Rebounds After 3 Months Of Declines
data.description:
September 2024 saw the seasonally-adjusted number of employed teens rise in the U.S. economy after having declined in each of the three previous months. The seasonally-adjusted number of employed teens Age 16-19 increased by 231,000 to 5,624,000.

Most of that increase took place among younger teens Age 16-17, whose numbers increased 159,000 to 2,193,000. Older teens Age 18-19 experienced a much smaller increase, with their totals increasing by 71,000 to 3,398,000.

If you're following along at home, you'll notice the numbers for each of these demographic subgroups don't quite add up to the total for the combined group. That's because the Bureau of Labor Statistics applies unique seasonal adjustment factors to each of these data series. If you want numbers that do add up, you'll need to pull the non-seasonally adjusted data for each demographic group. When you do, you'll find the following for the initial employment estimates for September 2024:

  • Age 16-17: 2,217,000 (a decrease of 58,000 from August 2024)
  • Age 18-19: 3,141,000 (a decrease of 367,000 from August 2024)
  • Age 16-19: 5,357,000 (a decrease of 425,000 from August 2024)

The reported total for the Age 16-19 is 1,000 less than what you would get by adding numbers of employed 16-to-17 year olds to the number of employed 18-to-19 year olds, but that's attributable to rounding errors resulting from the BLS' practice of reporting the employment numbers to the nearest thousand.

Although all the changes in the unadjusted employment figures are negative, the seasonally adjusted numbers are all positive because the decline in September 2024's teen payrolls was smaller than is typical for the regular seasonal pattern for teen employment. Teen employment spikes upward from May through July each year before falling back in August through October as many teens leave the workforce and return to school after their summer break. The seasonal adjustments applied by the BLS take this regular pattern for teen employment into account in smoothing out the seasonal swings in the number of employed teens

The following pair of charts shows the trends for the seasonally adjusted teen employment and the teen employment-to-population figures from January 2016 through September 2024.

Both charts show that even with the seasonally-adjusted rebound, teen employment levels in September 2024 are well off their peaks from earlier in the year. As rebounds go, it's welcome but not enough to offset the declines.

References

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Labor Force Statistics (Current Population Survey - CPS). [Online Database]. Accessed: 14 September 2024.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan Q3 Earnings: The Market Is Not Impressed
data.description:
Earlier this week, Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX) reported its Q3 2024 results and the market did not seem impressed. The stock is actually down by 1.3% over the past 5 trading days and is up nearly 13% on a year-to-date basis at a time when the S&P 500 delivered returns of almost twice that rate.

Although the market did not react to the earnings release, there were some noteworthy developments over the past 3-month period.

Earnings improved by 16% year-on-year on the back of higher copper's average realized price as copper production fell. Thus, FCX's stock remains driven by changes in copper price and operational developments within the company are just a nuisance at this point.

All As Expected

For the most part, the earnings call involved topics that FCX investors are well-aware of. From the ongoing expansion in Indonesia and the expected strong demand for copper due to electrification to the need to improve efficiencies in the U.S. to tackle the impact of lower grades and lastly growth opportunities from the company's leach initiatives.

All these topics are important for long-term investors, but nothing materially has changed during the quarter. For example, incremental copper production for the company's highly innovative leaching process increased by nearly 70% during the quarter.

At Freeport, we benefit from a large reserve and resource position with near term, medium term and longer term embedded growth options. The high potential innovative leach technologies are delivering results and we've several ongoing initiatives to add scale and low cost copper volumes in our Americas business. In the first nine months of 2024, the incremental copper production from our leach initiative was nearly 70% higher than the comparable period last year, and we have additional projects underway to build scale and improve on our US cost position.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

An impressive achievement to tackle the problem of low-grade ores, but that was already expected, and thus the copper sales outlook did not change.

Expected sale volumes of gold and molybdenum also remained unchanged during the quarter, as the price of the precious metal has skyrocketed in recent months. These two segments, however, remain a small fraction of FCX sales.

Management has also announced a slight improvement in cash costs from the July estimate, which brings the FY 2024 outlook roughly in-line with the one provided at the beginning of the year.

We also have updated our estimates for our unit net cash cost. We expect our average for 2024 to approximate $1.58 per pound. That is below our July estimate of $1.63 per pound and similar to our guidance at the start of the year of $1.60 per pound.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

Overall, the quarterly earnings release did not offer any material changes to FCX's near-term outlook, which now puts the emphasis back on copper prices.

All Eyes On Copper Prices

The long-term thesis for copper prices is indeed attractive. Demand for copper is expected to increase on the back of electrification trends and the need for more investments within electrical grids around the globe.

The problem for FCX shareholders, however, is that owning the stock is associated with many other operational and financial risks that could easily distort future returns, even if eventually copper prices skyrocket.

FCX management continues to expect a tightly balanced market in the near term and deficit conditions beyond that, which creates an appealing narrative around copper mining stocks. The near-term risks, however, are significant, and they highlight the need for almost perfect market timing when trying to take advantage of long-term trends through mining stocks.

Copper prices are very sensitive to changes in expectations about the global economy, and this, in my view, makes FCX uninvestable at the moment. The risks for the global economy continue to pile-up, and forecasting future actions by central banks around the globe is a futile exercise.

(...) where prices traded, they largely followed macro sentiment, which weighed global economic data and economic pressures in China against actions by the Fed and other central banks to cut interest rates and the potential for large economic stimulus in China.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

The yield curve continues to steepen which historically is a strong signal of a potential recession ahead and the Conference Board Leading Economic Index (LEI) is once again signalling woes for the U.S. economy ahead.

The unprecedented stimulus measures in China are also not a very encouraging signal for the health of the world's second-largest economy.

The copper to gold ratio is also near all-time lows, which a clear indication of the current risks for the global economy.

The reason why the copper to gold ratio is so important is because higher copper prices are associated with optimistic expectations about the economy, while higher gold prices are a strong signal of elevated risks for the global monetary system.

That is why, historically, we have seen a strong relationship between the copper to gold ratio and the 10-year bond yields, which fall during periods of time when economic stimulus is needed.

This relationship, however, has been broken as yields skyrocketed in recent year and are now at their highest levels since 2007.

In a nutshell, copper and gold prices are signalling more troubles ahead for the economy as long-term rates continue to increase after years of record-low levels.

All that creates significant short to medium term risks for the economy and even though the long-term thesis for copper prices might seem attractive, the downside risk is still too high to ignore.

Conclusion

The market did not seem impressed with Freeport-McMoRan's third quarter results for FY 2024. In spite of some positive developments during the quarter, the outlook remains unchanged. Progress is made in certain areas, such as Indonesia's expansion and costs, but for the time being investors remain laser focused on movements in copper prices. In that regard, risks still outweigh potential benefits.

data.title: Apollo Investor Day's Mysteries
data.description:
Apollo Global (NYSE:APO) held its Investor Day on October 1. The presentation was more than four hours long and included 200+ slides. Instead of reviewing it, I will limit myself to only several points that seemed intriguing to me.

Before we proceed, I want to remind you that there are two ways to invest in Apollo - either through its common stock APO or through its mandatory convertible preferred stock (NYSE:APO.PR.A).

The latter pays $3.375 per annum dividends and will be converted into APO on 7/31/2026. As long as APO trades above $98.97, the conversion ratio will be 0.5052 of APO shares per one APO.PR.A. Since the market is pretty certain about the "as long as" condition, the preferred stock trades slightly above 0.5052*APO (~ $72.75 at the time of writing) due to its downside protection in the unlikely case of APO's deep drop and to compensate for its higher dividend.

Newcomers can review my multiple posts on Apollo for the company's description, which I will skip here.

The Market Responds To Investor Day

Below is APO's chart from SA:

My modest contribution to the slide above is two arrows: the stock's drop on Aug 1 - the day of Apollo's Q2 earnings release, and the stock's jump on Oct 1 - the Investor Day.

On August 2, I published "Apollo's stock punished for a less than stellar quarter - nothing to worry about". The title describes what happened, though the market's reaction was out of proportion. The stock started climbing back until it jumped on Investor Day and has further advanced since, trading at an all-time high.

And here comes my question: what impressed investors so much on Investor Day?

In my August 2nd publication, I estimated Apollo's intrinsic value at $90-123. Granted, I strive to be conservative with my estimates. Still, the quick APO's ascend to $145, i.e. significantly above my fair value range, surprised me more than the drop after the Q2 release because I did not discover anything unexpected in the presentation except for some secondary details.

Investors were more likely to respond to the strategic plan of CEO Marc Rowan's presentation than to anything else. So, we will focus on it, and below is the central slide of his presentation.

The targets on the right side of the slide seemed very expected to me and in my posts, I often shared these expectations, emphasizing the most important figure of ~20% FRE growth. This number is the most influential on the stock price and that is why it is at the very top. Other numbers, besides SRE growth, serve only to provide for FRE growth. (By the way, SRE growth of 10% seems rather modest to me in the probable case of rating agencies further upgrading Athene.)

Back to the question I posed earlier. In my opinion (and I am not confident here), there are two reasons for the stock's jump - a microreason and a macro reason.

The microreason is presented on the least important slide of Mr. Rowan's presentation:

The slide explains relatively modest Q2 numbers caused by hedging of floating rate securities. In 2023, they overperformed, but are likely to underperform once interest rates start going down. Once Mr. Rowan attributed hedging costs to 2023 instead of Q2 2024 when they were incurred, Q2 numbers started looking good.

All this number-shuffling seems rather meaningless to me, especially since this issue was discussed in detail on Athene's Q2 earnings call! (Athene's earnings calls are separate from Apollo's and present a lot of valuable info.) Perhaps, the market is not that efficient after all...

The macro reason is revealed below:

The slide illustrates the total addressable market for opportunities in front of Apollo and its peers. Nobody can be certain about the precision of the numbers but their enormity impresses nonetheless. Even though I was familiar with all these opportunities, until Investor Day, I had never seen them succinctly packed on one slide. Perhaps, it helped the investing crowd to recognize what might lie ahead. Big alt managers are likely to grab an out-of-proportion fraction of these immense opportunities, as has already happened:

Private investments and the index

A part of Mr. Rowan's presentation was devoted to the comparison of public and private equity investments. He was rather critical of public markets. Read this, for example:

10 stocks are 39% of the S&P, 4 stocks have determined 100% of their returns for the last few years. And I jokingly say sometimes, we levered the entire retirement of America to Nvidia's performance. It just doesn't seem smart. We're going to fix this and we are in the process of fixing it...

The 8,000 public companies that used to form diversified portfolios are now 4,000 public companies. Fewer than 100 companies go public every year, more than 100 companies go private every year. We take for granted in the technology ecosystem that companies like OpenAI or Spotify can stay private a very long time and raise equity.

His ideas are clear and widely recognized: several big companies dominate the market, the market cannot deliver anything but beta, mutual funds underperform, and private investments will deliver alpha. Fair enough.

But how is it compatible with the following slide from his presentation?

Suddenly, we realize that despite denouncing the search for alpha in public equities, Mr. Rowan acknowledges that public Apollo has delivered this alpha and is hopeful that this will remain the case. He did not state the latter directly, but I think clearly implied it. Otherwise, his investment thesis for Apollo does not make much sense.

Bizarre responses by the market to Apollo's messages that we discussed in the previous section confirm that public markets, despite AI and everything else, are far from perfectly efficient. Perhaps, one can still invest at appropriate moments into the public operators of private investments (i.e. Apollo and other alt managers) and outperform the index? The simple fact that during two months from Aug 1 to Oct 1, APO jumped almost 50% without any significant news, confirms that public appraisal of alt managers is hardly efficient.

Conclusion

There was one other slightly mysterious moment in Mr. Rowan's presentation. It is well known that KKR (KKR) and Brookfield (BN) (BAM) cloned Apollo's insurance strategy. Mr. Rowan never mentioned them, but still shared his opinion with us. First, he mentioned that one of Apollo's competitors did everything well and then, speaking about Athene's competitive advantages, stated the following:

But suffice it to say, if you do not have ratings and you do not have service and you do not have this, what you're doing is you're buying secondary blocks of business with degraded surrender charges and degraded protection, and you are paying more for them than originating new business. Not a great way to run...

If you don't have these things, and most people who are entering the business and most traditional companies don't have any of these things, what do you do? Well, what you do is you take assets and liabilities to Cayman. You put up $0.50 on capital. You avoid a bunch of rules. And that's what we're watching. Any time someone tells you they're taking their business to Cayman, read that is short lived and lacking in any of these things.

Whom do you think he had in mind when dropping these words? I think it is rather obvious - all you need is to generalize Cayman, which, of course, is not a geographical name here, into other offshore locations such as Bermuda, for example...

After the jump, Apollo stock has become less attractive and is outside my fair value range. Shortly, it will deliver Q3 results, but they may not be stellar either due to the same reason - floating fixed income hedging. At least, it was directly mentioned on the last Athene earnings call. In Q4, the company is expected to resume back to normal, which means quick growth.

I remain optimistic about Apollo in the long term but will change my rating to "Hold" due to quick appreciation.

data.title: Turn Around Tuesday Comes Late
data.description:
Overview

It is ironic that a few months ago, many wanted to sell the dollar because the Republican president and vice president candidates said they wanted a weaker dollar. With the election drawing near and the race very tight, there has been a surge in the betting markets of a Trump-Vance victory, and this has corresponded with the dollar's dramatic rise. US rates held on the lion's share of their gains despite the sharpest loss in the S&P 500 since early September to cap the three-day slide. Still, the greenback is consolidating today, and seeing some of its recent gains pared against all the G10 currencies. And all but a few Asia Pacific currencies and the Russian ruble are firmer, too.

Corrective forces are also evident in global stock and bond markets. Asia Pacific equities were mostly lower, following the losses in the US yesterday. But helped by stronger earnings reports (see Tesla (TSLA), for example) US S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures are 0.4%-0.7% higher and Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is also snapping a three-day drop. Bonds are being bought too. Japan and Australian 10-year benchmark yields slipped almost two basis points, while European yields are off 3-4 bps. The UK Gilts are an exception. There, the yield is up nearly three basis points. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is off more than five basis points to 4.19%, leaving it still 11 bps higher on the week. Gold appeared to post a key reversal yesterday by setting a record high and then closing below the previous day's low. However, there has been no follow-through selling, and gold has recovered from $2715.55 at yesterday's close to approach $2740 today. December WTI is at an eight-day high, pushing above $72.20.

Asia Pacific

Since the low set on September 30 near JPY141.65, the US dollar has surged more than 8% against the yen, which, if sustained, would be the largest monthly gain since August 1995. From early June through early July, the greenback rallied about 4.8% and Japanese officials climbed the intervention ladder to large scale, initially unconfirmed intervention. We argue that what has made BOJ intervention appear effective is that officials sold dollars in a falling US rate environment. This was true in intervention in September 2022 and earlier this year. A consensus appears to have been formed that no matter the US election results, a larger budget deficit will result and higher US inflation. The US 10-year yield has risen by nearly 65 bps since mid-September to almost 4.25%. Although, we argue that due to its prior views and positioning, the market exaggerated the significance of the stronger-than-expected September employment data. Even if we are right that the October report will be more consistent with the underlying trend of a gradual slowing in job growth, the proximity of the US election and related concerns may prevent a pullback in US yields. Nevertheless, with the weaker yen no longer supporting Japanese equities, verbal intervention should be expected. Meanwhile, neither the Japanese nor Australian markets typically react much to the preliminary October PMI, and today is true to form. While Japan's manufacturing PMI remained below the 50 boom/bust level for the fourth consecutive month, the composite has been above 50 consistently this year, but for June. In October, it fell to 49.4 from 52.0. In its new economic projections, the IMF shaved its GDP forecast for Japan to 0.3% from 0.4%. In contrast, Australia's composite PMI remained below 50 for the second consecutive month (49.8 from 49.6). Despite the weakness of the yen, there is practically no chance of a Bank of Japan rate hike next week. The Reserve Bank of Australia meets on November 5, and it is unlikely to be swayed by today's PMI.

The dollar has surged by about 3% against the yen this week. It has nearly met the (61.8%) retracement of the intervention/US rate-inspired decline from the multiyear-high in early July (~JPY162) to the low in mid-September (briefly below JPY140). Speculators in the futures market swung from a net short position of 185k contracts in early July to net long 66k contracts in late September. They have been liquidating over the past few weeks, and price action is both a cause and effect of trend followers and momentum traders bailing out. There has been hardly a peep from Japanese officials about the price action. Both Finance Minister Kato and BOJ Governor Ueda had opportunities in the past 24 hours to comment on the yen but refrained. The strong three-day rally has stalled, and the dollar is pulling back from almost JPY153.20, yesterday's high, and has slipped below JPY152 in Europe. Nearby support may be in the JPY151.25-40 area. That shelf the Australian dollar was forging around $0.6655? It was taken out yesterday. The Aussie was sold to almost $0.6620 and settled below the 200-day moving average for the first time since mid-August. It is better bid today and has recovered to $0.6660 before pausing. There is a large option at $0.6605 (~A$1.55 bln) that expires today. The relentless rise of the dollar and yen slump is softening the Chinese yuan, even if there is greater foreign demand for Chinese shares. The dollar approached CNH7.1470 yesterday. As the dollar pulled back against the yen today, it has also pulled back against the yuan and approached CNH7.1165. The PBOC fix has been creeping up. It was set at CNY7.1286 today (CNY7.1245 yesterday).

Europe

The eurozone and UK's preliminary October PMI were uninspiring. The eurozone composite ticked up to 49.7 from 49.6. It was the second consecutive month below 50. So far, the year's peak was in May (52.2). Last October it was at 46.5. Germany's flash PMI was firmer and the composite rose to 48.4 from 47.5. The French results were softer, and the composite PMI fell to 47.3 from 48.6. The swaps market has about a 40% chance that the ECB cuts rates by a half-point at the December meeting, which, practically unchanged on the day, is slightly lower than at the end of last week. It still seems exaggerated. That said, another negative print on CPI (due next week) could change the assessment. The swaps market has slightly less than 125 bps of cuts through H1 25. In comparison, the Fed funds futures is discounting about 108 bps from the Federal Reserve. The swaps market has 112 bps of easing by the Bank of England over that period. The UK's preliminary October preliminary composite PMI slipped to 51.7 from 52.6 in September. It has not been below 50 since October 2023 when it was 48.7.

The euro took another leg lower yesterday and frayed the uptrend line connecting the April and June lows but managed to settle slightly above it (~$1.0765). It recovered to nearly $1.0810 in the European morning, which seemed to have exhausted the buying and the intraday momentum indicators warn of the risk of a pullback, with initial support in the $1.0770-80 area. There are options for 1.35 bln euros that expire today at $1.0755. Sterling frayed its trendline connecting the April and August lows found near $1.2910 yesterday, which met the (50%) retracement of this year's gains around $1.2930. Yesterday's low held today, and sterling recovered to $1.2975. Its intraday momentum indicators are more constructive than the euro, and the session high may not be in place. It may draw closer to $1.30.

America

US rates and the dollar are unlikely moved by today's data. It consists of the preliminary October PMI, where manufacturing has been below 50 for the past three months, weekly jobless claims (overshadowed by next week's national employment report), and September new home sales (existing home sales unexpectedly fell for the second consecutive month). Instead, the market is focused on the dramatic rise in US rates on ideas regardless of the election outcome, supply of Treasuries will increase, and inflation will rise. The measure of bond market volatility (MOVE) is at new highs for the year. The Bank of Canada delivered the 50 bp cut that was largely discounted, though it did not prevent the Canadian dollar from extending its losses. Its economic forecasts were unchanged from July. It expected the economy to grow by 2.1% in 2025 with 2.2% CPI. Governor Macklem characterized the economy as "excess supply," with "soft" labor market. Mexico reports inflation for the first half of October. The weaker peso may have lifted the headline inflation by the most in three months (~0.4%), The core rate has typically been firmer in the first half of a month than the second half. This pattern has held in all but three months since the start of last year. The swaps market has about a 1-in-3 chance of a cut discounted over the next month. The meeting is November 14.

The combination of a 50 bp Bank of Canada rate cut and the broader risk-off environment weighed on the Canadian dollar yesterday, but it slipped around 0.15%, making it the strongest of the G10 currencies. Still, the greenback reached new two-and-half-month highs near CAD1.3865. It is consolidating today and is likely to find support ahead of CAD1.3775. As the Loonie was the strongest in the G10, the Mexican peso was the strongest among the emerging market currencies yesterday, rising 0.5%. The greenback initially reached almost MXN20.10 before reversing lower and falling through MXN19.86. It is the third time in the last five sessions that the dollar traded above MXN20.00 on an intraday basis, and has been unable to close above it once. The greenback held above Tuesday's low (~MXN19.77) yesterday but has taken it out today. It fell to almost MXN19.7550 before finding bids in the European morning. Nearby resistance may be near MXN19.85.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Only Second Bank Failure This Year
data.description:
There has been a second bank failure in 2024.

Yes, so far...that's all.

And, this bank failure looks like there was something dishonest going on.

The failure was First National Bank of Lindsay, of Lindsay, Oklahoma. On June 30, 2024, it had $108 million of assets.

This was not the usual bank failure.

This was an unexpected and sudden closure.

The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency said it closed First National after finding “false and deceptive bank records and other information suggesting fraud.” The OCC added that the bank “was in an unsafe or unsound condition to transact business” and that its assets were less than its obligations.

A lot of uninsured depositors stand to lose a lot of money.

The OCC is bringing in the Justice Department to investigate the situation.

The interesting thing to me is that this is only the second bank closing this year.

The other failure was in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

Republic First Bank was quite a bit bigger than the First National Bank of Lindsay; it had $6 billion in assets.

Also, the uninsured depositors of Republic First Bank did not face the same problem that faced the uninsured depositors of First National Bank of Lindsay.

The bank that acquired Republic First Bank assumed all of the deposits of the bank...including the uninsured ones. One could say that it was a pretty clean takeover and that although the bank failed, the primary reason for the failure was the changes that had taken place in interest rates.

The major point that I want to make, however, is not the bank failure itself, but the fact that through roughly ten months in 2024, there have only been two bank failures so far in the calendar year!

This, to me, says a lot about what the Federal Reserve is doing and what it has been doing over the past 2 1/2 years or so.

Over the past 2 1/2 years, the Federal Reserve has been conducting a policy of monetary tightening. The specific policy approach it has been following it titled "quantitative tightening."

For the past 2 1/2 years or so, the Federal Reserve has overseen a reduction in its securities portfolio.

The reduction has been steady and expected.

The banking system has been prepared for the Fed's actions, announced in advance of execution.

The Fed has reduced its securities portfolio by more than $1.0 trillion.

The Federal Reserve had purchased a lot more securities than just $1.0 trillion as it responded to the impacts of the Covid-19 pandemic and subsequent recession.

And, current plans are for the Fed to continue reducing the size of the securities portfolio into the near future.

But, the point is that the Fed has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio for 2 1/2 years and has announced that it will continue to program further into the future.

So, in the almost 10 months of this year, and after almost thirty months preceding this year's 10 months, only two banks have failed.

I am calling attention to this outcome because it is a result of the monetary policy strategy devised by former Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke and speaks to some of the strengths of this particular policy strategy.

In the past, the Federal Reserve has attempted to reverse periods in which excessive amounts of liquidity have been provided to the banking system, the central bank has moved rapidly and adjusted aggressively to get the banking system back into a "more normal" situation.

The efforts to move quickly and reverse the monetary injection generally caused a major disruption of the banking system along with many bank failures.

The "new" Bernanke program has apparently avoided all of this kind of disruption.

Mr. Bernanke made sure that bank management's knew what the Fed was trying to do, knew that the adjustments would occur over an extended period so that the banks would have time to adjust more smoothly, and knew that they could trust the Fed in sticking to the program.

As a result of this approach the banking system appears to be adjusting to the Fed's efforts, the financial system seems to have accepted what the Federal Reserve is doing, and the economy seems to be rolling along at a pretty acceptable pace.

In other words, there have been very few bank failures.

The stock market has hit a very large number of new historic highs this year.

And, the economy looks as if it will grow by at least 2 1/2 percent in the coming year.

This is a fantastic record. And, the hope is that the Federal Reserve will be able to continue what it is doing into the near future with the same success.

Perhaps the most important lesson from this "new" monetary policy, a policy that focuses upon steadily moving quantities, is that monetary policy needs to be constructed in a way that avoids many, radical shifts over relatively short periods of time.

Focusing primarily on interest rate targets in the past resulted in financial markets and institutions that were volatile and that accepted many, many bank failures.

What the Federal Reserve has done with this "new" policy is to take a lot of the uncertainty out of what it will be doing for an extended period of time.

And, if the Federal Reserve can earn the trust of the banks, then banking adjustments will be smoother because the Federal Reserve policy will be known and will be lived up to.

Again, it is truly remarkable to me that the Federal Reserve has been conducting a policy of quantitative tightening for more than 30 months.

It is truly remarkable to me that the economy has been growing steadily during this time of quantitative tightening and is expected to continue to grow through this next year.

It is truly remarkable to me that inflation has dropped to a level that is approaching the Fed's target rate of inflation of 2.0 percent.

And, it is truly remarkable that the Fed was able to operate for a decade...the 2010s...using several periods of "quantitative easing" where the real economy grew at a compound rate of about 2.3 percent, and where inflation remained down at a compound rate of about 2.3 percent.

Furthermore, the banking system seems to be adjusting to this "new" approach to monetary policy and seems to be operating with fewer and fewer failures.

Not a bad outcome, Mr. Bernanke.
